title: blender keeps running in podman if killing it forcefully
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

Forcefully killing blender using CTRL+C | +/ inside podman keeps blender running.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3028982/153246249-cf186fbf-4ecf-4b2f-9524-64bbee9c41df.png)

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

```
podman run --rm --entrypoint bash docker.io/nytimes/blender:3.0-gpu-ubuntu18.04 -c "wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/zodtv/examples/main/blender/bootstrap.sh && chmod +x bootstrap.sh && ./bootstrap.sh https://gateway.ipfs.io/ipfs/QmYhEsVWdzbCU49XE8F9ybKKAPQHjhfhekoMn4Xvz857rD SIASKY OPTIX 1080 1350 1"

Run a blender image and wait until it gets to the step of rendering using blender.

Ctrl+C | +/ kill blender.
```

**Describe the results you received:**
Blender is running after podman is killed.

**Describe the results you expected:**
All child pids spawned inside podman after it is killed should be killed.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
Podman crashes with a stacktrace

comments.body:
Podman sends the TERM signal to the pid1 of the container, if the container process ignores it , then there is nothing that Podman can do.

You could do a `podman stop $CID` in a separate terminal, which will stop the container with a SIGKILL after 10 seconds.

> Podman sends the TERM signal to the pid1 of the container, if the container process ignores it , then there is nothing that Podman can do.
> 
> You could do a `podman stop $CID` in a separate terminal, which will stop the container with a SIGKILL after 10 seconds.

I dont see why podman cant send the `podman stop` by intercepting CTRL+C//. This seems like a common way to shoot your foot (run long running computation, see something is wrong, kill said computation, its still running)

Also note the callstack, it means podman itself crashed. That should not happen under any circumstance.
Podman hands off the terminal handling and does not monitor what is going on.  BTW Docker works the same way.

I would like to add that if `podman run` is called and the caller dies (say running podman from inside script then script dies) podman keeps running.  Does that mean instead of podman run maybe something like podman exec needs to be used?

While I agree with Dan that we can’t do more than forward signals, the
stacktrace there looks worth investigating and fixing, Podman should not
panic

On Thu, Feb 10, 2022 at 16:31 vans163 ***@***.***> wrote:

> I would like to add that if podman run is called and the caller dies (say
> running podman from inside script then script dies) podman keeps running.
> Does that mean instead of podman run maybe something like podman exec needs
